coredata.dc:title: A flower-like g-C3N4/CDs/Bi2WO6 hierarchical structure for enhanced photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline

coredata.dc:description: Tetracycline (TC), as one of antibiotic emerging pollutants, has posed potential threats to ecological environment and human health because it is hardly biodegradable and prone to be accumulated in water. In this study, g-C3N4/CDs/Bi2WO6 S-scheme heterojunction with flower-like hierarchical structure was synthesized by hydrothermal method. The optimized composite demonstrates significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity with a TC degradation efficiency of 90.1% after 120 min of visible light irradiation and good reusability up to four consecutive cycles. This can be attributed to the improved light capture capability and efficient separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs in the constructed S-scheme heterojunction. The introduced carbon dots (CDs) can be served as an efficient carrier transport highway, providing a superior photogenerated carrier separation efficiency and an expanded absorption spectrum. As a result, the strongest redox potentials of the CBCN and VBBWO can be retained for participating the photocatalytic reaction. Free radical capture and electron spin resonance experiments indicate that ·O2 - and h+ are the principal active species in the g-C3N4/CDs/Bi2WO6 system. This work provides new strategy for the development of advanced materials in the field of photocatalytic treatment of wastewater.

head.ce:title: A flower-like g-C3N4/CDs/Bi2WO6 hierarchical structure for enhanced photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracycline (TC), as one of antibiotic emerging pollutants, has posed potential threats to ecological environment and human health because it is hardly biodegradable and prone to be accumulated in water. In this study, g-C3N4/CDs/Bi2WO6 S-scheme heterojunction with flower-like hierarchical structure was synthesized by hydrothermal method. The optimized composite demonstrates significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity with a TC degradation efficiency of 90.1% after 120 min of visible light irradiation and good reusability up to four consecutive cycles. This can be attributed to the improved light capture capability and efficient separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs in the constructed S-scheme heterojunction. The introduced carbon dots (CDs) can be served as an efficient carrier transport highway, providing a superior photogenerated carrier separation efficiency and an expanded absorption spectrum. As a result, the strongest redox potentials of the CBCN and VBBWO can be retained for participating the photocatalytic reaction. Free radical capture and electron spin resonance experiments indicate that ·O2 - and h+ are the principal active species in the g-C3N4/CDs/Bi2WO6 system. This work provides new strategy for the development of advanced materials in the field of photocatalytic treatment of wastewater.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, the widespread use of antibiotics in pharmaceuticals, clinical practices, aquaculture, and animal husbandry has caused substantial damage on aquatic environments, posing a significant threat to ecological balance [1–4]. However, traditional antibiotic removal technologies such as physical adsorption and chemical oxidation are limited by low efficiency and cost [5,6]. Photocatalytic degradation technology, driven by solar energy, has been extensively researched due to the advantages such as high efficiency, environmentally benign, and sustainability [7–10]. Nevertheless, design and engineering of catalysts with superior photocatalytic performance remains a formidable challenge.

As a visible light-activated photocatalyst, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, abbreviated as CN) has emerged as a prominent material for pollutant elimination [11,12], CO2 reduction [13,14], and hydrogen production [15,16]. This is attributed to its inherent advantages such as appropriate band gap, high stability, nontoxicity and easy synthesis. However, the photocatalytic efficacy of CN is hindered by several drawbacks, including weak oxidation potential, low electronic conductivity, and rapid recombination of photo-generated electron-hole (e--h+) pairs [13,17–19].

Constructing heterojunction is considered an ideal strategy for significantly promoting the separation and transfer of photo-generated e--h+ pairs in photocatalysts [20,21]. For traditional Type Ⅱ heterojunction, the enhanced migration of photo-generated e--h+ pairs is achieved at the cost of sacrificing their high redox ability [22]. The conventional Z-scheme heterojunction can retain the strongest oxidation-reduction ability of photo-generated e- and h+, however, there are some drawbacks. For example, redox couples are sensitive to external pH values, prone to side reactions, and compete with photocatalysts for light capture [23–25].

The S-scheme heterojunction is constructed by reducing photocatalysts (RP) with higher Fermi energy levels (Ef) and oxidizing photocatalysts (OP) with lower Ef. Due to their different work functions, the built-in electric field formed at the interface can accelerate the recombination of photo-generated e- in the CB of OP and h+ in the VB of RP, which helps to retain photo-generated e- and h+ in the CB of RP and the VB of OP, respectively, thereby maintaining the strongest redox potential in the heterojunction system [18,26]. Therefore, selecting appropriate semiconductors and constructing S-scheme heterojunctions with g-C3N4 may be of great significance in enhancing intrinsic photocatalytic activity.

Bismuth tungstate (Bi2WO6, abbreviated as BWO), in particular, shows great promise in photocatalysis due to its unique layered structure and excellent chemical and physical stability. Notably, the positive valence band edge of BWO is conducive to forming a staggered band structure with other semiconductors with negative conduction band edges, which can maintain its strong oxidation potential and promote the transfer of photo-generated charges [27–29]. For example, Zhao et al. reported a n-p type Bi2WO6/AgInS2 S-scheme heterojunction with excellent photocatalytic activity, which can more effectively degrade norfloxacin and levofloxacin compared to the corresponding monomers [30]. The S-scheme Cs2AgBiBr6/Bi2WO6 nanocomposites synthesized by Wang et al. exhibited excellent activity in photocatalytic CO2 reduction to produce CH4 and CO [31]. Given the well-matched band positions between CN and BWO, it stands to reason that constructing a S-scheme heterojunction would effectively accelerate the separation of photo-generated charges.

As an emerging class of carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered attention for their potential to augment photocatalytic activity when coupled with semiconductors. This interest is largely due to their photogenerated electron transfer properties and distinctive up-converted photoluminescence characteristics [32,33]. Xu et al. prepared S-scheme ZnIn2S4/CDs/g-C3N4 (ZIS/CDs/CN) heterojunction photocatalysts using CDs as solid electronic media. Compared with ZIS and CN, the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution efficiency was significantly enhanced, attributed to the synergistic effect between CDs and S-scheme heterojunction [34]. Ji et al. prepared Bi4O5I2 hollow nanotubes modified by nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). N-CQDs doping in the catalysts effectively accelerated the surface charge migration of Bi4O5I2, improving the photocatalytic performance for the degradation of bisphenol A [35]. Therefore, employing CDs as conduits for photogenerated electron transfer in S-scheme heterojunction is anticipated to yield a superior photogenerated carrier separation efficiency and an expanded absorption spectrum. Such advancements are expected to significantly enhance the efficiency of antibiotic removal, leveraging the synergistic effects of these materials in the photocatalytic process.

In this study, we successfully synthesized CDs-mediated S-scheme heterojunction CN/CDs/BWO using hydrothermal method and investigated its photocatalytic performance for TC degradation under visible light irradiation. Based on free radical capture experiments, electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy analysis and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, the photocatalytic mechanism of CN/CDs/BWO composite was elucidated. As expected, the CN/CDs/BWO composite demonstrated superior TC degradation efficiency compared to BWO, CN, CDs/CN and CN/BWO counterparts, highlighting its potential for effectively removing organic pollutants from wastewater.

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O), acetic acid (CH3COOH), melamine, ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), tetracycline (C22H24N2O8, TC), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), 1,4-benzoquinone (p-BQ) and isopropanol (IPA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the reagents were used without any additional purifications.

Preparation of carbon dots (CDs) [33]: 16.7 mmol of urea and 15.6 mmol of citric acid were fully dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water and then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180°C for 5 h. Upon completion of the reaction, large particles were removed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. The CDs were then harvested through freeze-drying.

Preparation of CN: 80 mmol of melamine and 750 mmol of NH4Cl were thoroughly ground and calcined in a muffle furnace at 550°C for 4 h. The heating rate was maintained at 2.3°C/min. After cooling to room temperature, CN was obtained.

Preparation of CN/CDs/BWO composite: Initially, a specified amount of CDs and CN were uniformly dispersed in a mixture containing 2 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 10 mL of acetic acid through ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Afterwards, Na2WO4 aqueous solution (2 mmol Na2WO4·2H2O in 35 mL deionized water) was slowly added dropwise into the mixture and stirred at room temperature for 2 h. The obtained suspension was subsequently transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 160°C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was washed sequentially with deionized water and ethanol several times and dried at 80°C for 12 h. For comparison, binary catalyst CN/BWO, CDs/CN and pure BWO were also prepared using the same method, omitting the corresponding component.

The photocatalytic performance of the synthesized catalysts was assessed through the degradation of TC under visible light irradiation and a 300 W xenon lamp (λ≥400 nm) was used as the light source. The procedure is briefly described as follows: catalyst (25 mg) was dispersed into 50 mL TC solution with a concentration of 20 mg/L. Prior to irradiation, the suspension was stirred for 30 min to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Afterward, the mixture was exposed to the light source with stirring. 3 mL of solution were extracted from the reactor at 30 min intervals and centrifuged to collect the supernatant. The concentration of TC was determined using a UV-visible spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 357 nm. After the degradation experiment, the catalyst was dried and recovered for subsequent experiments.

The structural phases of the synthesized catalysts were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation on a BRUKER D8 ADVANCE Diffractometer. The microstructures of the catalysts were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM 7800F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100F). Additionally, Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) mapping images were obtained at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV for compositional analysis of the samples. Surface elemental composition and chemical states of the catalysts were investigated using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi) with Al Kα radiation. The chemical bonds of the catalyst were characterized by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS 10). UV-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (Cary 7000) was utilized to characterize the light absorption properties of the catalysts, using BaSO4 as a reference. Specific surface area of the catalysts was calculated based on N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms by using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. The ZETA potential of the catalyst was recorded using a ZETA potential and nanoparticle size analyzer. To identify reactive oxygen species, Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) technology was employed using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMPO) as capture agents for ·OH, h+, and ·O2 - in ultrapure water and methanol, respectively. Ultimate 3000 UHPLC-Q liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS, Thermo Scientific) was conducted to determine the intermediate products.

The photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the catalysts were measured using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation in a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution. The experiments employed a conventional three-electrode system comprising: a working electrode, a counter electrode (platinum electrode), and a reference electrode (saturated Ag/AgCl electrode). The working electrode was constructed using fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) conductive glass coated with a sample film.

The preparation of the working electrode was conducted as follows: 5 mg of the photocatalyst was uniformly dispersed in a mixture of 100 μL ethanol and 10 μL naphthol (5 wt%, Sigma-Aldrich). The suspension was then coated on the conductive surface of the FTO glass and left to dry naturally overnight.

All periodic DFT calculations were conducted based on the Vienna ab initio simulations package (VASP) [36–38]. A 2 × 2 × 1 supercell (96 O atoms, 16 W atoms, and 32 Bi atoms) was built as bulk unit of Bi2WO6. The supercell has a dimension of 10.87 Å × 16.43 Å × 10.91 Å. The surface slab cleaved from (200) surface of bulk unit cell was used as the Bi2WO6 slab model. The stationary phase g-h-heptazine is used as the calculation model of g-C3N4. A vacuum thickness of 20 Å with periodic boundary conditions was set to avoid the interactions between periodically-repeated layers. The Projector Augmented Wave (PAW) [39,40] method was utilized to describe the core and valence electrons. The electron exchange correlation terms were described using the Generalized Gradient Approximation (GGA) and Perdew Burke Ernzerhof (PBE) [41] functions. Kinetic energy cutoff of the set plane wave was 500 eV. The convergence achieved at the energy difference is 10−5 eV, with an ion relaxation convergence standard of 0.01 eV/Å. The K point of the Brillouin region is set to 3 × 3 × 1 in the calculation of the structure. To account for van der Waals (vdW) interactions, the DFT-D3 scheme with an empirical damped potential term was added to the electronic energy [42].

The crystal phases of the synthesized catalysts were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD). The results, as depicted in Figs. 1 and S1, reveal the characteristic diffraction peaks of BWO and CN. Specifically, the main peaks at 28.3°, 32.8°, 47.1°, 55.9°, 58.5°, 68.8° and 75.8° can be assigned to the (131), (200), (331), (262), (400), (311) and (204) crystal planes of orthogonal Bi2WO6 (JCPDS 39-0256) [27], respectively. The diffraction pattern of CN aligns with that of graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, JCPDS 87-1526) [8]. The CN/BWO and CN/CDs/BWO samples exhibit diffraction peaks similar to those of BWO. Notably, the diffraction peaks corresponding to CN and CDs are absent in the CN/BWO and CN/CDs/BWO composites. This observation can be attributed to the poor crystallinity of CN and CDs, as well as the possibility that the diffraction peaks of CN are overshadowed by those of BWO [43].

The structural composition of the synthesized catalysts was analyzed using FT-IR spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 2 , the peaks of BWO located between 500 cm-1 and 800 cm-1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of Bi-O and W-O bonds, respectively [28,44]. In the case of CN, the absorption bands ranging from 1200 to 1700 cm-1 and 3000 to 3500 cm-1 correspond to the typical stretching vibration modes of C-N heterocyclic compounds and the stretching vibration modes of adsorbed H2O or residual N-H, respectively. The distinct absorption peaks at 806 cm-1 and 885 cm-1 are indicative of the breathing vibration mode of s-triazine units and the deformation mode of N-H [45,46]. For CDs, the peak at 1545 cm-1 is associated with C=C functional group, and the absorption band near 1662 cm-1 is related to C=O bond of -COOH group [35]. Interestingly, the FT-IR spectrum of CN/CDs/BWO composite exhibits absorption bands characteristic of both CN and CDs, confirming the successful integration of CN and CDs in the CN/CDs/BWO heterojunction, which is crucial for its photocatalytic functionality.

The surface elemental composition and chemical states of CN, BWO, and the CN/CDs/BWO composite were investigated by using XPS, which are presented in Fig. 3 . In Fig. 3a, the CN/CDs/BWO composite is confirmed to comprise elements Bi, O, W, C, and N. The Bi 4f spectrum and W 4f spectrum of CN/CDs/BWO, as shown in Fig. 3b and 3c, both exhibit a decrease in the binding energies by comparing to those of pure BWO [47,48]. The O 1s peak of BWO (Fig. 3d) can be deconvoluted into two peaks centered at 529.95 eV (Bi-O) and 530.86 eV (W-O), which shift towards a higher binding energy for the CN/CDs/BWO composite [49]. In the N 1s spectrum of the composite (Fig. 3e), the peaks of C-N=C, N-(C)3, and C-N-H shift towards higher binding energies in comparison with those of CN [18,21,44]. Meanwhile, the binding energies of C-C, sp3-coordinated carbon species, C=O, and N=C-N in the C 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 3f) show a similar variation [21,43]. These shifts in binding energies suggest a strong interaction between BWO and CN in the composite [5,28,50], which is beneficial to the effective separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, implying its enhanced photocatalytic capabilities.

The micro-morphology and structural characteristics of the catalysts were examined using SEM and TEM, which are illustrated in Figs. 4 and S2. Fig. 4a shows the SEM image of BWO, revealing its three-dimensional (3D) flower-like structure composed of nanosheets. In Fig. 4b, CN is observed to have a smooth, curly sheet-like morphology. For CN/CDs/BWO and CN/BWO (Figs. 4c and S2a), flower-like morphology in a smaller size with hierarchical multilayer structure is presented, illustrating the intimate contact between BWO and CN. EDX mapping (Figs. 4g and S2c) further confirms that the elements C, N, Bi, W, and O are uniformly distributed across the composite, indicating the successful construction of heterojunction. TEM images displayed in Figs. 4d, 4e and S2b reveal that CDs, approximately 5 nm in size, are scattered on the surface of CN. This facilitates the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic activity of CN/CDs/BWO. In addition, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 4f) shows a lattice spacing of 0.27 nm, which is attributed to the (200) plane of BWO [27].

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were conducted to determine the specific surface areas of BWO, CN and the CN/CDs/BWO composite. As depicted in Fig. 5 , all samples exhibit Type IV adsorption-desorption isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, indicating the presence of mesoporous structure in the photocatalysts [51]. The CN/CDs/BWO composite demonstrates a larger specific surface area (34.17 m²/g) compared to pure BWO (17.4 m²/g), which is conducive to enhancing the photocatalytic performance by providing more active sites.
The photocatalytic performance of the synthesized catalysts in TC degradation at various conditions were systematically evaluated, the results are presented in Figs. 6 and S3.

As illustrated in Figs. 6a and S3a, CN exhibits the lowest photocatalytic efficiency of 70% even though it possesses the largest specific surface area (60.18 m²/g, Fig. 5) among the samples, suggesting its inherent limitation of rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. This observation indicates that, in addition to the specific surface area, other factors might play a crucial role in determining the photocatalytic performance of a photocatalyst. By constructing CN/BWO heterojunction, the photocatalytic activity can be significantly improved due to the enhanced separation efficiency of photo-generated carriers. The removal efficiency of TC reaches 83.4% after visible light irradiation for 120 min. It is interesting that incorporating CDs into CN/CD/BWO further enhances the photocatalytic performance, achieving the highest TC degradation efficiency of 90.1%, which is comparable to other photocatalysts reported in literature (Table S1). This superior performance can be primarily ascribed to the role of CDs as efficient electron transporters, facilitating the rapid transfer of photo-generated electrons. However, due to photon competition absorption, excessive CDs may have adverse effects on the photocatalytic performance of CN/CD/BWO (Fig. S3b).

Moreover, to further compare the photocatalytic efficiency of different catalysts, the pseudo-first-order kinetic model in Eq. (1) was used to calculate the apparent rate constant.
(1)
− ln
(
C t
C 0
)
= kt
where Ct is the TC concentration at different reaction time (t), C0 denotes the initial TC concentration, and k is the apparent rate constant.

In Fig. S4, good linearity is observed between -ln(Ct/C0) and t. The degradation rate constant (k) of TC (0.0483 min-1) over CN/CDs/BWO is 2.7, 2.4, 1.7 and 1.5 times those over CN, BWO, CDs/CN and CN/BWO, respectively.

Fig. 6b depicted the effect of the initial TC concentration on the photocatalytic activity of the CN/CDs/BWO composite. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 6b that the degradation efficiency of TC decreases from 90.1% to 75.2% as the initial concentration of TC increases from 20 mg/L to 50 mg/L. Despite this reduction, CN/CDs/BWO maintains high catalytic efficiency over a wide range of initial TC concentrations. The decrease in performance can be attributed to two factors: firstly, the higher initial concentration of TC leads to more intermediate products competing with reactive oxygen species, thereby reducing catalytic activity. Secondly, the increased concentration of TC intensifies competition for the active sites on the catalyst [52].

The presence of inorganic anions in natural water matrices may also affect the activity of photocatalysts [21,26]. As shown in Fig. 6c, the effect of different anions (10 mM for each solution) on the photocatalytic degradation of TC can be ordered by H2PO4 - >> Cl- > SO4 2- > NO3 -. The strongest inhibitory effect of anion H2PO4 - might be attributed to its rapid capture of h+ and ·OH, leading to the lowest degradation rate of TC (73.4% after 120 min of light irradiation) [53,54]. Moreover, varying the initial pH of the TC solution remarkably influences the photocatalytic performance of CN/CDs/BWO, as illustrated in Fig. 6d. This is because the pH affects both the surface charge properties and the adsorption capacity of the catalyst. TC exists in different forms depending on the pH: as a cation in solutions with pH < 3.3, as a zwitterion in solutions within the pH range of 3.3∼7.7, and as an anion in solutions with pH > 7.7 [55]. At pH 3.0, both TC and the catalyst are positively (negatively) charged (Fig. S5), leading to electrostatic repulsion that inhibits photocatalytic activity. The TC degradation increases at pH 5 (and 9) due to reduced electrostatic repulsion. The highest photocatalytic efficiency is observed at pH 7.0, where TC is zwitterionic, facilitating electrostatic attraction between TC and the catalyst.

The photocatalytic performance of CN/CDs/BWO was further explored by degrading ciprofloxacin (CIP), ofloxacin (OFL), bisphenol A (BPA) and rhodamine B (RhB). As shown in Fig. S6, the photocatalytic degradation efficiencies of CIP, OFL, BPA and RhB within 120 min can reach 82.4%, 92.2%, 78.6% and 100%, respectively, demonstrating the potential application of CN/CDs/BWO in efficient degradation of diverse organic pollutants.

Recycling experiments for photocatalytic TC degradation under visible light irradiation were conducted to assess the reliability of CN/CDs/BWO composite, the results are presented in Fig. 6e and 6f. As shown in Fig. 6e, after four cycles, the degradation efficiency of TC can still reach up to 84.3%, indicating high catalytic stability of the composite. Furthermore, no significant changes were observed in the XRD pattern and SEM image of the used CN/CDs/BWO compared to the fresh sample (Figs. 6f and S7), confirming its structural stability. Thus, CN/CDs/BWO exhibits not only excellent photocatalytic removal of organic pollutants but also high stability, making it a promising material for environmental remediation. In addition, to evaluate the potential environmental implications of the photocatalyst itself, the concentrations of leached metal ions (Bi3+ and W6+) from CN/CDs/BWO after photocatalytic degradation were determined by Inductively Coupled Plasma Spectroscopy. The concentrations of Bi3+ and W6+ are only 266.512 µg/L and 125.943 µg/L, respectively, implying that metal leaching is negligible and the obtained composite is environmentally friendly.

In summary, the CN/CDs/BWO composite exhibits excellent photocatalytic performance such as high activity, high stability and wide applicability. However, it should be noted that the cumbersome preparation process of CDs and the difficulty in separating and recovering powder photocatalysts may limit the practical applications of CN/CDs/BWO in water treatment. These issues will be considered in our future research.

LC-MS was implemented to analyze the intermediate products of TC photocatalytic degradation process and 19 intermediate products (m/z=417, 403, 360, 338, 318, 301, 223, 219, 174, 165, 151, 136, 118, 108, 88, 74 and 64) were detected (Fig. S8). As shown in Fig. 7 , based on LC-MS intermediates and previous reported results [56–58], three possible TC degradation pathways have been proposed. For pathway Ⅰ, intermediates P1(m/z=417) and P2(m/z=403) are formed through demethylation of TC. P2 undergoes dehydration, demethylation, oxidative ring opening and deamination, resulting in P3(m/z=318) and P7(m/z=219). Subsequently, P7 is subjected to ring opening oxidation and dehydroxylation to form P8(m/z=165) and P9(m/z=136). In the second pathway, intermediates P4(m/z=360), P5(m/z=338) and P6(m/z=301) are formed through demethylation, dehydroxylation, deamidation and deamination of P1. Among them, P6 undergoes further reactions such as deacetylation and oxidation to obtain P7. In the third route, intermediate P6 undergoes ring cleavage reaction to form P10(m/z=223), which is further converted into P11(m/z=174) and P12(m/z=151). Finally, all the intermediate products mentioned above are decomposed into small molecules, CO2 and H2O under the action of free radicals.

The efficiencies of photogenerated carrier transport in CN, BWO, and the CN/CDs/BWO composite were investigated using Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS), as illustrated in Fig. 8 a. Obviously, CN/CDs/BWO exhibits the smallest impedance radius compared to the other samples, indicating more efficient carrier migration in the CN/CDs/BWO composite [59,60]. To further substantiate the reduced recombination rate of photogenerated e--h+ pairs in CN/CDs/BWO, transient photocurrent response test was also conducted, the results are shown in Fig. 8b. As it can be seen, all samples display stable photocurrent intensities. In particular, CN/CDs/BWO demonstrates the strongest photocurrent intensity, confirming that integrating CDs as an electronic medium at the interface of CN and BWO effectively enhances the separation efficiency of photogenerated e--h+ pairs. The consistency between these electrochemical results and the observed photocatalytic performance reinforces the conclusion that the structural and compositional design of CN/CDs/BWO plays a pivotal role in its enhanced photocatalytic capabilities.

The optical absorption characteristics of the catalysts were analyzed using UV-Vis DRS. The results are presented in Fig. 9 a. The absorption edges of BWO and CN were observed to be approximately 443 nm and 537 nm, respectively. Interestingly, the light absorption capacity of CN/BWO is similar to that of BWO alone, indicating that enhanced light absorption is not the sole factor contributing to the improved photocatalytic activity of CN/BWO. However, a notable redshift in the light absorption edge is observed for CN/CDs/BWO (482 nm) compared to CN/BWO [33,35]. This enhancement in the light absorption capability of CN/CDs/BWO confirms the incorporation of CDs, which aligns well with the experimental results showing increased TC photodegradation under visible light irradiation.

The bandgap energies (Eg) of CN and BWO were calculated using the Tauc plots, based on the equation αhν = A(hν - Eg)n, where α is the absorption coefficient, hν the photon energy, A a constant, and Eg the bandgap energy. For these calculations, n was set to 2, reflecting the indirect electron transitions in both BWO and CN [61]. As shown in Fig. 9b, the Eg values for BWO and CN were determined to be 2.92 eV and 2.73 eV, respectively, which are in line with previously reported values in literature [29]. Notably, the Eg value for CN/CDs/BWO was calculated to be 2.88 eV, indicating an improved visible light absorption ability in comparison with CN/BWO. Furthermore, the valence band (VB) potentials of CN and BWO, derived from the intersection of the extension lines of the straight part of the XPS VB spectra [62], are found to be 1.5 eV and 2.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 9c and 9d). Consequently, the conduction band (CB) potentials of CN and BWO are calculated as -1.23 eV and 0.12 eV by using the formula Eg = EVB - ECB.

To elucidate the photocatalytic mechanism of CN/CDs/BWO, the primary active species involved in the photodegradation process were identified using free radical trapping experiments. In these experiments, IPA, p-BQ, and EDTA-2Na were employed as scavengers for determining the specific contributions of ·OH, ·O2 - and h+ to the decomposition of TC, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 10 a, the addition of p-BQ or EDTA-2Na to the TC solution severely inhibits the photocatalytic activity of CN/CDs/BWO. The TC removal efficiency sharply reduces to 59.6% and 55.3% in each case, respectively. However, the presence of IPA in the TC solution only slightly decreases the removal efficiency to 86%. These findings suggest that h+ and ·O2 - of the CN/CDs/BWO composite play critical roles in the photocatalytic reaction, while ·OH contributes to a lesser extent, which can be further confirmed by ESR characterization (Fig. 10b–d). Fig. 10b and 10c show that no signal was detected for the CN/CDs/BWO composite in the dark, while both the characteristic resonance signals of DMPO-·O2 - with a 1:1:1:1 intensity pattern and DMPO-·OH with a 1:2:2:1 intensity pattern are observed under visible light irradiation. However, the signal for DMPO-·OH presents no change with extended irradiation time, indicating the limited generation of ·OH during the photocatalysis. In Fig. 10d, the appearance of TEMPO-h+ signal peaks either in the dark or under irradiation strongly confirms the generation of h+, though the intensity of these peaks decreases over irradiation time due to the oxidation of h+ by TEMPO [63].

Based on HRTEM/XRD results, The CN (002) and BWO (200) crystal planes were constructed (Fig. S9), and density functional theory (DFT) was used to calculate and evaluate the work functions of CN and BWO to infer charge transfer modes. As shown in Fig. 11 , the Fermi level (Ef) of CN is higher than that of BWO, and the work functions of CN (002) surface and BWO (200) surface are 4.43 eV and 5.28 eV, respectively. When CN and BWO come into contact, a balanced Ef can be achieved since the electrons in CN spontaneously migrate to BWO. Consequently, the BWO side and CN side at the interface exhibit negative and positive charges respectively due to the accumulation and loss of electrons, leading to band bending and the formation of a built-in electric field from CN to BWO [18,26,43,63]. Therefore, the binding energies of Bi3+, W6+ and O2 - were decreased with an increase in the binding energy of C and N, which is consistent with the XPS results (Fig. 3). Moreover, the resulted band bending facilitates the recombination of photogenerated electrons in the CBBWO and holes in the VBCN at the interface and retains the strongest redox potentials of the CBCN and VBBWO, which is crucial to the efficient photocatalytic degradation of TC.

Based on the obtained band structure, work function and the identified active species in the CN/CDs/BWO composite, we deduce that the S-scheme heterojunction was successfully constructed. The possible mechanism of TC photocatalytic degradation under visible light was proposed, as shown in Fig. 12 . Specifically, under visible light irradiation, CN and BWO are excited to produce e- and h+, respectively when absorbing energy greater than the bandgap energy. The built-in electric field, band bending, and Coulomb interaction are beneficial for the recombination of photo-generated electrons in CB of BWO and holes in VB of CN, leaving holes in VB of BWO and enriching electrons in the CB of CN. More importantly, CDs play a role of efficient carrier transportation highways, facilitating the transfer and recombination of photo-generated e- in the CB of BWO and the photo-generated h+ in the VB of CN on the surface of CDs. This S-scheme heterojunction mechanism not only accelerates the separation and transfer efficiency of photo-generated charges, but also retains excellent redox ability. This significantly improves the photocatalytic activity of CN/CDs/BWO composite materials.

In summary, CDs acting as solid-state electron mediators for S-scheme CN/CDs/BWO composite has been successfully prepared through a hydrothermal method and used for photocatalytic degradation of TC. Compared to CN, BWO and binary composite materials, CN/CDs/BWO exhibits the highest photocatalytic performance and excellent reusability. Experimental characterizations and theoretical calculations indicate that the enhanced photocatalytic performance of CN/CDs/BWO can be attributed to the broadened photo-response range and improved redox ability. The incorporation of CDs accelerates the separation efficiency of photo-generated e--h+ pairs in the CN/CDs/BWO and promotes the photocatalytic degradation of TC under the attack of h+ and ·OH and ·O2 -. Furthermore, based on LC-MS analysis, three possible degradation pathways of TC were speculated. This work not only demonstrates the effective degradation of antibiotics using the CN/CDs/BWO composite but also provides new insights and perspectives for the development of advanced materials in the field of photocatalytic water treatment.
